content:
One Script. Countless Problems.

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Juanjo Jaramillo on UnsplashThere was one Python file in our repository that everyone avoided.
It wasn’t the biggest file. It wasn’t the oldest either. In fact, if you opened it, the code looked surprisingly ordinary. A few functions. Some API calls. A handful of helper utilities. Nothing screamed “danger.”
Yet every developer on the team knew the rule.
Don’t touch it.
Nobody remembered who originally wrote it. The commit history had become a timeline of tiny fixes, formatting changes, and comments saying things like “Temporary workaround” that somehow survived for years. Every feature in our automation pipeline eventually flowed through this single file.
Deleting it felt like pulling the first brick from a bridge.
I kept asking the same question.
Why?
After four years of building Python automation projects, I’ve learned that the most dangerous code is rarely the most complicated. It’s usually the code that quietly became responsible for everything.
That realization completely changed how I write automation.
